text.title: ES Versioning Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules grounding event-schema evolution + GDPR PII handling.

rule.statement: Once an event class has been published (consumed by any downstream system, or persisted in any production stream), its field contract is immutable. Field renames, removals, or semantic changes are forbidden without a version bump.
rule.rationale: Silent edits break every consumer that decoded the old shape; outages are deterministic and embarrassing.

rule.statement: Every published event class MUST have an `event_version` integer field (default 1). Any field rename, removal, or semantic change MUST bump `event_version` AND register an upcaster transforming old-shape into new-shape on read.

rationale.source: Greg Young — Versioning in Event-Sourced Systems

rule.rationale: Upcasters allow consumers to receive the latest shape transparently; without them every consumer needs version-aware decoders forever.

rule.statement: A committed `event-catalog.yml` (or equivalent schema-registry export) MUST live in the repository. Each event class has an entry: `{name, version, fields[], upcaster_from?}`. CI MUST verify the catalog matches the code.
rule.rationale: The committed catalog is the auditable contract; without it, version history is lost.

rule.statement: CI MUST fail when a published event class changes without (a) a version bump and (b) an upcaster from the previous version. The catalog file MUST be edited in the same PR as the event class change.
rule.rationale: CI gate is the only reliable enforcer; reviewers miss silent renames in busy PRs.

rule.statement: PII (names, addresses, emails, card tokens) MUST NOT appear in event payloads unless one of: (a) per-subject crypto-shredding key stored in a mutable keystore (drop key on erasure request), (b) opaque ID in the event with PII kept in a mutable GDPR-compliant side table.
rule.rationale: Immutable event log cannot satisfy erasure directly; crypto-shredding or externalization is the only compliant pattern.
